title: ALL (Acute Lymphoblastic Leukaemia)
oneLiner: Aggressive haematological malignancy of lymphoid precursors; most common childhood cancer, less common in adults; requires immediate specialist referral.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Neutropenic sepsis:** temperature ≥38°C (or unwell with rigors) in a patient with known or suspected neutropenia (ANC <0.5). **Key finding:** hypotension or altered mental status in a febrile patient — do not wait for cultures, start empiric IV antibiotics immediately (piperacillin-tazobactam per local policy).
- **Tumour lysis syndrome (TLS):** develops within 12–72 hours of starting chemotherapy (or spontaneously in high-burden disease). **Key finding:** acute oliguria, tetany, or ECG changes (peaked T-waves, widened QRS) due to hyperkalaemia. Check urate, phosphate, calcium, LDH, and K+ urgently.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Pallor, fatigue, exertional dyspnoea (anaemia)
  - Fever, recurrent infections (neutropenia)
  - Bruising, petechiae, epistaxis, gum bleeding (thrombocytopenia)
  - Bone pain (especially long bones, sternum) — often worse at night, may be migratory
  - Lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly (may be asymptomatic)
  - Headache, vomiting, visual disturbance (if CNS involvement — rare at presentation in adults, more common in children)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Pallor, petechiae, purpura
  - Lymphadenopathy: firm, non-tender, may be generalised (cervical, axillary, inguinal)
  - Splenomegaly, hepatomegaly
  - Sternal tenderness
  - Signs of infection: focus may be absent (neutropenic fever)
  - Neurological signs if CNS disease: cranial nerve palsies, papilloedema
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Acute myeloid leukaemia (AML):** presents similarly but has Auer rods on blood film, myeloperoxidase (MPO) positivity, and different immunophenotype (CD13/CD33+). **Splitter:** presence of lymphoblasts on blood film (ALL) vs. myeloblasts (AML).
- **Aplastic anaemia:** pancytopenia but no circulating blasts, no organomegaly, bone marrow biopsy shows hypocellularity, not blast infiltration.
- **Immune thrombocytopenia (ITP):** isolated thrombocytopenia with normal haemoglobin and WCC; no blasts on film, no bone pain.
- **Infectious mononucleosis (EBV):** fever, lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, but positive Monospot test, atypical lymphocytes (not blasts), self-limited.
- **Myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS):** older adults, cytopenias with dysplastic features on marrow, <20% blasts.
- **Non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL) with leukaemic phase:** presents with bulky lymphadenopathy, but blasts may be seen; immunophenotyping differentiates (e.g., B-ALL vs Burkitt lymphoma).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Full set of observations (NEWS2)
  - ECG (for TLS: hyperkalaemia, hypocalcaemia)
  - Urine dipstick + urine output monitoring
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC + blood film (blast count, anaemia, thrombocytopenia)
  - Coagulation screen (may be abnormal in DIC)
  - U&Es, creatinine, LDH, urate, phosphate, calcium (TLS risk)
  - LFTs (liver infiltration, chemotherapy impact)
  - Blood cultures (febrile patient)
  - Virology: HIV, hepatitis B/C, EBV, CMV (pre-treatment screening)
  - Group and save / cross-match
  - Pregnancy test (in females of childbearing age)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Chest X-ray:** mediastinal mass (T-ALL), infection
  - **CT neck-chest-abdomen-pelvis:** staging, assess disease burden, identify complications (e.g., splenic rupture, lymph node compression)
- **Special tests:**
  - Bone marrow biopsy + aspirate: definitive diagnosis — morphology, flow cytometry (immunophenotyping), cytogenetics (e.g., Philadelphia chromosome, MLL rearrangements), molecular genetics
  - Lumbar puncture: CSF cytology + flow cytometry (CNS involvement)
  - Immunophenotyping: defines subtype (B-ALL: CD19, CD20, CD10; T-ALL: CD7, CD3)
  - Cytogenetics: t(9;22) = Philadelphia chromosome (poor prognosis in adults, treated with tyrosine kinase inhibitors)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** WHO classification requires ≥20% lymphoblasts in bone marrow or peripheral blood (in practice, diagnosis often made on blood film + flow cytometry, confirmed on marrow).
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood film** — if pancytopenia with blasts, treat as acute leukaemia until proven otherwise. A missed diagnosis delays life-saving therapy.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Call haematology registrar on-call
  - If febrile neutropenia: start empiric IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or per local policy) within 1 hour; take cultures first but do not delay
  - IV fluids for TLS prophylaxis: 2–3 L 0.9% saline / 24 hours (check fluid balance, avoid overload if renal impairment)
  - Allopurinol 300 mg PO once (or rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg IV if high TLS risk, per local policy)
  - Monitor ECG, K+, urate, phosphate
  - Transfuse if: Hb <70 g/L (or <80 g/L if symptomatic), platelets <10 (<20 if fever, <50 if invasive procedure)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Admit to haematology ward (or isolation room if neutropenic)
  - Reverse barrier nursing (neutropenic precautions) if ANC <0.5
  - Start chemotherapy per protocol (e.g., UKALL14/17 for adults, UKALL 2011 for children) — usually 4-drug induction: vincristine, prednisolone, anthracycline (daunorubicin), PEG-asparaginase — exact regimen per haematology team
  - Monitor tumour lysis syndrome: check U&Es, urate, phosphate, calcium, LDH every 6–12 hours during first 72 hours
  - If Ph+ ALL: add tyrosine kinase inhibitor (imatinib or dasatinib) per protocol
  - CNS prophylaxis: intrathecal methotrexate ± cytarabine (per protocol, may begin during induction)
  - Antiemetics, PPI (proton pump inhibitor) prophylaxis (steroids + chemotherapy)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Consolidation, re-induction, maintenance phases (2–3 years total therapy)
  - Allogeneic stem cell transplantation (SCT) for high-risk disease (e.g., Ph+ ALL, adverse cytogenetics, relapse)
  - Regular monitoring: FBC, BM assessments, minimal residual disease (MRD) by flow cytometry/PCR
  - Long-term follow-up for late effects (cardiotoxicity, secondary malignancies, endocrine)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 2–3 L 0.9% saline / 24h IV (adjust for age, comorbidities) — increased if TLS risk
  - VTE prophylaxis: low molecular weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless platelet count <50 or coagulopathy
  - Diet: hospital food (no special restrictions unless neutropenic: avoid raw/undercooked food)
  - Observations: 4-hourly (more frequent if febrile or unstable); daily weight, fluid balance, NEWS2
  - IV access: PICC line or central line (for chemotherapy administration)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (induction — per UKALL protocols):**
  - Vincristine: 1.4 mg/m² IV (max 2 mg) weekly — dose per local protocol
  - Prednisolone: 40–60 mg/m² PO daily (or dexamethasone 6 mg/m², per protocol) — taper over 4 weeks
  - Daunorubicin: 60 mg/m² IV days 1, 8, 15 — per protocol; [check local policy]
  - PEG-asparaginase: 1000 IU/m² IM/IV day 4 — per protocol; [check local policy]
  - Intrathecal methotrexate: 12 mg (age-dependent) on day 1 — [check local policy]
- **Ph+ ALL:** imatinib 400–600 mg PO daily (starting dose varies) — [check local policy]
- **Common alternatives:** cytarabine, cyclophosphamide, etoposide (salvage/consolidation)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Vincristine: dose reduce if bilirubin >50 µmol/L or hepatic impairment; neurotoxic, avoid concurrent neurotoxic drugs (e.g., isoniazid)
  - PEG-asparaginase: contraindicated in pancreatitis, severe hepatic impairment, history of thrombosis/haemorrhagic events
  - Prednisolone: may cause hyperglycaemia, hypertension, mood changes; use with caution in diabetes, peptic ulcer
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** most chemotherapy drugs require dose adjustment for renal or hepatic impairment — always refer to protocol and pharmacy. For example, daunorubicin dose reduction if bilirubin >20 µmol/L; methotrexate avoid in significant renal impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Vincristine — never give intrathecally** (lethal). Intrathecal methotrexate only to be given by trained personnel. Also, PEG-asparaginase dose miscalculation (units vs IU) can cause severe toxicity.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Tumour lysis syndrome (hyperkalaemia, hyperphosphataemia, hypocalcaemia, hyperuricaemia, AKI)
  - Neutropenic sepsis (bacterial, fungal)
  - Bleeding (thrombocytopenia, DIC)
  - Chemotherapy toxicity: vincristine neuropathy (constipation, foot drop), steroid-induced diabetes/psychosis, anthracycline cardiotoxicity, PEG-asparaginase pancreatitis/thrombosis
  - Mucosal barrier injury (mucositis)
  - CNS complications: intracranial haemorrhage, infection (especially if intrathecal therapy)
- **Chronic:**
  - Secondary malignancies (therapy-related AML/MDS)
  - Cardiotoxicity (anthracycline dose-dependent)
  - Avascular necrosis of bone (steroids)
  - Growth failure, infertility, endocrinopathies (children)
  - Chronic GVHD if transplant
  - Relapse (CNS, marrow, testicular)
sections.Prognosis: - **Children:** 5-year survival ~90% (low-risk), ~85% overall. **Adults:** 40–60% 5-year survival (worse with age, Ph+ status, high WCC at diagnosis). Relapse is commoner in adults. Allogeneic SCT improves cure rates in high-risk disease.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Check blood film on any patient with unexplained pancytopenia** — do not wait for haematology consult to review film; describe blasts in request.
- **Start allopurinol or rasburicase immediately** if TLS risk (high LDH, high blast count) even before chemotherapy is given.
- **Ensure pregnancy test is documented before chemotherapy** — teratogenic agents (methotrexate, cyclophosphamide) are contraindicated in pregnancy.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give antibiotics and then wait for cultures** in febrile neutropenia — delay is lethal. Give empiric broad-spectrum within 1 hour.
- **Do not transfuse platelets prophylactically if patient has DIC** unless life-threatening bleeding (may worsen thrombosis). Seek haematology advice.
- **Do not start lumbar puncture for CNS staging** if platelets <50 or coagulopathy — risk of spinal haematoma. Correct first.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home — R:
- **Return if:** fever ≥38°C (or feeling unwell with chills), new confusion, headache with stiff neck, severe abdominal pain, shortness of breath, or bleeding that does not stop.
- **Follow-up:** haematology clinic within 1 week; contact specialist nurse; provided with 24-hour helpline number (most cancer centres have this).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior haematology registrar for:**
  - Fever ≥38°C in neutropenic patient (ANC <0.5)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L
  - Platelet count <10 (or <20 if bleeding)
  - Potassium >6.5 mmol/L or rapid rise (TLS)
  - Suspected spinal cord compression (back pain, leg weakness, sphincter disturbance) — urgent MRI spine
  - Acute confusion, seizure, or focal neurology
  - Severe bleeding (e.g., haemoptysis, haematemesis, melaena)
- **Call crash team if:** NEWS2 ≥7, unresponsive, airway compromise, cardiac arrest.

rawMarkdown:
# Acute Lymphoblastic Leukaemia (ALL)

**One-liner:** Aggressive haematological malignancy of lymphoid precursors; most common childhood cancer, less common in adults; requires immediate specialist referral.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Neutropenic sepsis:** temperature ≥38°C (or unwell with rigors) in a patient with known or suspected neutropenia (ANC <0.5). **Key finding:** hypotension or altered mental status in a febrile patient — do not wait for cultures, start empiric IV antibiotics immediately (piperacillin-tazobactam per local policy).
- **Tumour lysis syndrome (TLS):** develops within 12–72 hours of starting chemotherapy (or spontaneously in high-burden disease). **Key finding:** acute oliguria, tetany, or ECG changes (peaked T-waves, widened QRS) due to hyperkalaemia. Check urate, phosphate, calcium, LDH, and K+ urgently.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Pallor, fatigue, exertional dyspnoea (anaemia)
  - Fever, recurrent infections (neutropenia)
  - Bruising, petechiae, epistaxis, gum bleeding (thrombocytopenia)
  - Bone pain (especially long bones, sternum) — often worse at night, may be migratory
  - Lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly (may be asymptomatic)
  - Headache, vomiting, visual disturbance (if CNS involvement — rare at presentation in adults, more common in children)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Pallor, petechiae, purpura
  - Lymphadenopathy: firm, non-tender, may be generalised (cervical, axillary, inguinal)
  - Splenomegaly, hepatomegaly
  - Sternal tenderness
  - Signs of infection: focus may be absent (neutropenic fever)
  - Neurological signs if CNS disease: cranial nerve palsies, papilloedema

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Acute myeloid leukaemia (AML):** presents similarly but has Auer rods on blood film, myeloperoxidase (MPO) positivity, and different immunophenotype (CD13/CD33+). **Splitter:** presence of lymphoblasts on blood film (ALL) vs. myeloblasts (AML).
- **Aplastic anaemia:** pancytopenia but no circulating blasts, no organomegaly, bone marrow biopsy shows hypocellularity, not blast infiltration.
- **Immune thrombocytopenia (ITP):** isolated thrombocytopenia with normal haemoglobin and WCC; no blasts on film, no bone pain.
- **Infectious mononucleosis (EBV):** fever, lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, but positive Monospot test, atypical lymphocytes (not blasts), self-limited.
- **Myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS):** older adults, cytopenias with dysplastic features on marrow, <20% blasts.
- **Non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL) with leukaemic phase:** presents with bulky lymphadenopathy, but blasts may be seen; immunophenotyping differentiates (e.g., B-ALL vs Burkitt lymphoma).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Full set of observations (NEWS2)
  - ECG (for TLS: hyperkalaemia, hypocalcaemia)
  - Urine dipstick + urine output monitoring
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC + blood film (blast count, anaemia, thrombocytopenia)
  - Coagulation screen (may be abnormal in DIC)
  - U&Es, creatinine, LDH, urate, phosphate, calcium (TLS risk)
  - LFTs (liver infiltration, chemotherapy impact)
  - Blood cultures (febrile patient)
  - Virology: HIV, hepatitis B/C, EBV, CMV (pre-treatment screening)
  - Group and save / cross-match
  - Pregnancy test (in females of childbearing age)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Chest X-ray:** mediastinal mass (T-ALL), infection
  - **CT neck-chest-abdomen-pelvis:** staging, assess disease burden, identify complications (e.g., splenic rupture, lymph node compression)
- **Special tests:**
  - Bone marrow biopsy + aspirate: definitive diagnosis — morphology, flow cytometry (immunophenotyping), cytogenetics (e.g., Philadelphia chromosome, MLL rearrangements), molecular genetics
  - Lumbar puncture: CSF cytology + flow cytometry (CNS involvement)
  - Immunophenotyping: defines subtype (B-ALL: CD19, CD20, CD10; T-ALL: CD7, CD3)
  - Cytogenetics: t(9;22) = Philadelphia chromosome (poor prognosis in adults, treated with tyrosine kinase inhibitors)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** WHO classification requires ≥20% lymphoblasts in bone marrow or peripheral blood (in practice, diagnosis often made on blood film + flow cytometry, confirmed on marrow).
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood film** — if pancytopenia with blasts, treat as acute leukaemia until proven otherwise. A missed diagnosis delays life-saving therapy.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Call haematology registrar on-call
  - If febrile neutropenia: start empiric IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or per local policy) within 1 hour; take cultures first but do not delay
  - IV fluids for TLS prophylaxis: 2–3 L 0.9% saline / 24 hours (check fluid balance, avoid overload if renal impairment)
  - Allopurinol 300 mg PO once (or rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg IV if high TLS risk, per local policy)
  - Monitor ECG, K+, urate, phosphate
  - Transfuse if: Hb <70 g/L (or <80 g/L if symptomatic), platelets <10 (<20 if fever, <50 if invasive procedure)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Admit to haematology ward (or isolation room if neutropenic)
  - Reverse barrier nursing (neutropenic precautions) if ANC <0.5
  - Start chemotherapy per protocol (e.g., UKALL14/17 for adults, UKALL 2011 for children) — usually 4-drug induction: vincristine, prednisolone, anthracycline (daunorubicin), PEG-asparaginase — exact regimen per haematology team
  - Monitor tumour lysis syndrome: check U&Es, urate, phosphate, calcium, LDH every 6–12 hours during first 72 hours
  - If Ph+ ALL: add tyrosine kinase inhibitor (imatinib or dasatinib) per protocol
  - CNS prophylaxis: intrathecal methotrexate ± cytarabine (per protocol, may begin during induction)
  - Antiemetics, PPI (proton pump inhibitor) prophylaxis (steroids + chemotherapy)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Consolidation, re-induction, maintenance phases (2–3 years total therapy)
  - Allogeneic stem cell transplantation (SCT) for high-risk disease (e.g., Ph+ ALL, adverse cytogenetics, relapse)
  - Regular monitoring: FBC, BM assessments, minimal residual disease (MRD) by flow cytometry/PCR
  - Long-term follow-up for late effects (cardiotoxicity, secondary malignancies, endocrine)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 2–3 L 0.9% saline / 24h IV (adjust for age, comorbidities) — increased if TLS risk
  - VTE prophylaxis: low molecular weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless platelet count <50 or coagulopathy
  - Diet: hospital food (no special restrictions unless neutropenic: avoid raw/undercooked food)
  - Observations: 4-hourly (more frequent if febrile or unstable); daily weight, fluid balance, NEWS2
  - IV access: PICC line or central line (for chemotherapy administration)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (induction — per UKALL protocols):**
  - Vincristine: 1.4 mg/m² IV (max 2 mg) weekly — dose per local protocol
  - Prednisolone: 40–60 mg/m² PO daily (or dexamethasone 6 mg/m², per protocol) — taper over 4 weeks
  - Daunorubicin: 60 mg/m² IV days 1, 8, 15 — per protocol; [check local policy]
  - PEG-asparaginase: 1000 IU/m² IM/IV day 4 — per protocol; [check local policy]
  - Intrathecal methotrexate: 12 mg (age-dependent) on day 1 — [check local policy]
- **Ph+ ALL:** imatinib 400–600 mg PO daily (starting dose varies) — [check local policy]
- **Common alternatives:** cytarabine, cyclophosphamide, etoposide (salvage/consolidation)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Vincristine: dose reduce if bilirubin >50 µmol/L or hepatic impairment; neurotoxic, avoid concurrent neurotoxic drugs (e.g., isoniazid)
  - PEG-asparaginase: contraindicated in pancreatitis, severe hepatic impairment, history of thrombosis/haemorrhagic events
  - Prednisolone: may cause hyperglycaemia, hypertension, mood changes; use with caution in diabetes, peptic ulcer
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** most chemotherapy drugs require dose adjustment for renal or hepatic impairment — always refer to protocol and pharmacy. For example, daunorubicin dose reduction if bilirubin >20 µmol/L; methotrexate avoid in significant renal impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Vincristine — never give intrathecally** (lethal). Intrathecal methotrexate only to be given by trained personnel. Also, PEG-asparaginase dose miscalculation (units vs IU) can cause severe toxicity.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Tumour lysis syndrome (hyperkalaemia, hyperphosphataemia, hypocalcaemia, hyperuricaemia, AKI)
  - Neutropenic sepsis (bacterial, fungal)
  - Bleeding (thrombocytopenia, DIC)
  - Chemotherapy toxicity: vincristine neuropathy (constipation, foot drop), steroid-induced diabetes/psychosis, anthracycline cardiotoxicity, PEG-asparaginase pancreatitis/thrombosis
  - Mucosal barrier injury (mucositis)
  - CNS complications: intracranial haemorrhage, infection (especially if intrathecal therapy)
- **Chronic:**
  - Secondary malignancies (therapy-related AML/MDS)
  - Cardiotoxicity (anthracycline dose-dependent)
  - Avascular necrosis of bone (steroids)
  - Growth failure, infertility, endocrinopathies (children)
  - Chronic GVHD if transplant
  - Relapse (CNS, marrow, testicular)

### Prognosis
- **Children:** 5-year survival ~90% (low-risk), ~85% overall. **Adults:** 40–60% 5-year survival (worse with age, Ph+ status, high WCC at diagnosis). Relapse is commoner in adults. Allogeneic SCT improves cure rates in high-risk disease.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Check blood film on any patient with unexplained pancytopenia** — do not wait for haematology consult to review film; describe blasts in request.
- **Start allopurinol or rasburicase immediately** if TLS risk (high LDH, high blast count) even before chemotherapy is given.
- **Ensure pregnancy test is documented before chemotherapy** — teratogenic agents (methotrexate, cyclophosphamide) are contraindicated in pregnancy.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give antibiotics and then wait for cultures** in febrile neutropenia — delay is lethal. Give empiric broad-spectrum within 1 hour.
- **Do not transfuse platelets prophylactically if patient has DIC** unless life-threatening bleeding (may worsen thrombosis). Seek haematology advice.
- **Do not start lumbar puncture for CNS staging** if platelets <50 or coagulopathy — risk of spinal haematoma. Correct first.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home — Rare in Acute Phase)
- **Return if:** fever ≥38°C (or feeling unwell with chills), new confusion, headache with stiff neck, severe abdominal pain, shortness of breath, or bleeding that does not stop.
- **Follow-up:** haematology clinic within 1 week; contact specialist nurse; provided with 24-hour helpline number (most cancer centres have this).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior haematology registrar for:**
  - Fever ≥38°C in neutropenic patient (ANC <0.5)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L
  - Platelet count <10 (or <20 if bleeding)
  - Potassium >6.5 mmol/L or rapid rise (TLS)
  - Suspected spinal cord compression (back pain, leg weakness, sphincter disturbance) — urgent MRI spine
  - Acute confusion, seizure, or focal neurology
  - Severe bleeding (e.g., haemoptysis, haematemesis, melaena)
- **Call crash team if:** NEWS2 ≥7, unresponsive, airway compromise, cardiac arrest.
